section: Enhanced mechanical properties of a Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel by increasing hot-rolling reduction

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the paper abstract and introduction. The abstract reports that increasing hot-rolling reduction from 45% to 82% refines grain size and raises dislocation density, yielding strength of 648 MPa YS, 976 MPa UTS, 50% TE, and 105 J/m² toughness. Strengthening is ascribed to grain boundary and dislocation contributions; slight ductility loss comes from faster slip band refinement saturation (~50 nm spacing) and earlier microband formation. The introduction reviews Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels, typical property ranges (YS>500 MPa, UTS>800 MPa, TE>40% for 15-30Mn-6-12Al-0.5-1.2C wt%), limitations of κ-carbide age hardening, and hot rolling grain refinement via dynamic recrystallization. The studied fully austenitic steel has nominal composition Fe-31Mn-9Al-1C-0.45Nb-0.45Mo-0.45W (wt%). The objective is to investigate hot-rolling reduction effects on microstructure and properties. Subsequent sections are expected to detail experimental methods, quantitative microstructural data, mechanical test curves, and discussion of strengthening and strain hardening mechanisms.
unresolved_terms: detailed hot-rolling parameters (pass number, temperature, quench)
quantitative grain size and dislocation density data

context_update.summary: The current chunk reports the experimental procedures and the first part of the microstructural characterization results for the Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel. After hot forging at 1150 °C to 110 mm billets, reheating, and hot rolling to three reduction ratios (45%, 68%, 82%), the plates were water quenched. The as-quenched microstructure is fully austenitic with equiaxed grains and a high density of Σ3 annealing twins. EBSD analysis (Fig. 1) provided grain sizes (without twins: 45.11, 27.02, 18.80 μm; with twins: 21.82, 11.55, 7.02 μm for HR45, HR68, HR82), Σ3 twin fractions (67%, 60%, 52%), and GND densities (2.71×10^13, 7.49×10^13, 9.87×10^13 m^-2). SEM (Fig. 2) revealed (Nb,Mo,W)C MC carbides (micron-sized polygonal, 1–25 μm; submicron spherical, ~260 nm, ~0.2 vol%). TEM (Figs. 3,4) confirmed their FCC structure with a=4.36 Å and identified nano-sized κ-carbides (L′12) with sizes ~0.8 nm and volume fractions ~3.3–3.9%. The density was 6.82 g/cm^3, ~13% lighter than pure iron. Mechanical property data (tensile and impact) and deformation substructure evolution have not yet been presented but are expected in subsequent chunks. Important to retain the sample identifiers (HR45, HR68, HR82), their processing parameters, and the quantitative microstructural baselines for interpreting upcoming structure–property relationships.
sample_aliases: HR45: 45% reduction, 60 mm final thickness, 4 hot-rolling passes
HR68: 68% reduction, 35 mm final thickness, 6 passes
HR82: 82% reduction, 20 mm final thickness, 9 passes
processing_route: Hot-forged 110 mm billet at 1150 °C, air cooled
Reheated to 1150 °C, hot rolled to 45%/68%/82% reduction, water quenched

microstructure: Fully austenitic (FCC), equiaxed grains with first-order annealing twins
Grain sizes (excluding twins): 45.11 μm (HR45), 27.02 μm (HR68), 18.80 μm (HR82)
Effective grain sizes (including twins): 21.82 μm, 11.55 μm, 7.02 μm
Micron-sized (Nb,Mo,W)C: 1–25 μm, polygonal, aligned along RD
Nano-sized κ-carbides (L′12): sizes ~0.78–0.83 nm, volume fractions ~3.3–3.9%
properties: Density 6.82 g/cm^3, ~13% mass reduction vs pure iron
figures_or_tables: Fig. 1: EBSD BC, phase+GB, KAM maps for HR45/68/82
Fig. 2: SEM BSE images of second-phase particles
Fig. 4: TEM SAED and DF images of κ-carbides
unresolved_terms: Mechanical properties (tensile curves, strength, ductility, toughness)
SFE value not reported in this chunk

section: 3.1. Examination of microstructure at different hot-rolling reductions

context_update.summary: This chunk completes the microstructure characterization of the as-quenched hot-rolled samples by confirming nano-sized κ-carbide precipitation via TEM (Fig. 4) and reports the mechanical property data. TEM SAED and dark-field images identified (Fe,Mn)3AlC-type L′12 κ-carbides with sizes of ~0.8 nm and volume fractions of ~3.3–3.9%, varying little with reduction. The chunk then presents room-temperature tensile curves (Fig. 5a, Table 1), Charpy impact energies (Fig. 5b), and fracture surface observations (Fig. 6). With increasing hot-rolling reduction from 45% to 82%, YS and UTS rose while uniform and total elongations decreased moderately; reduction of area remained >60%, and Charpy impact energy remained >100 J/m². Fracture was ductile dimple type; intergranular (Nb,Mo,W)C particles did not degrade toughness because stress concentrations were relieved by intragranular particle debonding and microvoid formation. Dimple size correlated with grain size. Finally, the HR82 sample is benchmarked against other austenitic steels (Fig. 7), showing a superior combination of strength, ductility, and toughness without aging. The following sections are expected to quantitatively analyze strengthening contributions and strain-hardening mechanisms, linking the disclosed property trends to the previously characterized grain structure and dislocation densities.
material_systems: Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel (HR45, HR68, HR82)
sample_aliases: HR45: 45% hot-rolling reduction, final thickness 60 mm
HR68: 68% hot-rolling reduction, final thickness 35 mm
HR82: 82% hot-rolling reduction, final thickness 20 mm
processing_route: Hot forging at 1150 °C to 110 mm billet; reheating to 1150 °C; hot rolling to 45%, 68%, or 82% reduction; water quenching
key_variables: κ-carbide size (~0.8 nm) and volume fraction (3.3–3.9%)
microstructure: nano-sized (Fe,Mn)3AlC κ-carbides with L′12 structure, homogeneous intragranular precipitation
properties: HR82 yield strength ~650 MPa, UTS ~971 MPa, TE 50%, Charpy impact energy 105 J/m²
strength–ductility–toughness combination superior to aging-hardened low-density steels
mechanisms: stress concentration at intergranular (Nb,Mo,W)C alleviated by intragranular particle debonding and microvoid formation
larger grain size leads to larger dimples
figures_or_tables: Fig. 4: TEM SAED and DF of κ-carbides
Fig. 7: property comparison with other austenitic steels
Fig. 3: TEM-EDS and SAED of submicron (Nb,Mo,W)C particle

context_update.summary: This Discussion section (4.1) quantitatively analyzes how hot-rolling reduction influences microstructure and mechanical properties. It attributes grain refinement to enhanced dynamic recrystallization and particle-stimulated nucleation, and reports increasing GND density. Using Eq.5–9, yield strength contributions are separated: κ-carbide shearing gives ~60 MPa (HR45 60.16, HR68 58.04, HR82 60.98 MPa); (Nb,Mo,W)C Orowan bypassing contributes only ~6–7 MPa and is essentially constant, so precipitate strengthening does not drive the strength increase. Dislocation strengthening rises from 56.29 to 107.50 MPa with reduction, while grain boundary strengthening (from high-angle and twin boundaries) dominates, increasing from 157.02 to 276.78 MPa. Hall-Petch fitting with effective grain size including twins yields σ0=203.10 MPa and kHP=733.43 MPa·μm^1/2. Calculated sums match experimental YS (Table 2). The discussion emphasizes that grain boundary and dislocation strengthening are the primary mechanisms for the YS improvement. These quantitative contributions and parameters will be relevant for subsequent strain-hardening and ductility analysis.
processing_route: hot rolling with 45%, 68%, 82% thickness reductions, water quenched
microstructure: equiaxed austenite grains with annealing twins
figures_or_tables: Fig. 8 (grain size, dislocation density, particle size/fraction)

context_update.summary: This chunk completes Section 4.1 with Fig. 9(b) and Table 2, confirming grain boundary and dislocation strengthening as the dominant mechanisms for yield strength increase with reduction. Section 4.2 then analyzes strain-hardening behavior and deformation substructure evolution. The steel shows a three-staged strain-hardening rate (drop, rise, decrease) with a sustained ~2000 MPa plateau, contributing to high ductility. Despite a high SFE of ~73 mJ/m², planar dislocation glide dominates due to κ-carbide-induced glide plane softening, forming slip bands, Taylor lattices, and later microbands. Interrupted tests at ε_e=0.1, 0.2, 0.4 reveal slip band spacing values (e.g., HR45: 296.40±50.06 nm, HR68: 155.02±38.42, HR82: 68.05±8.91 nm at 0.1) and that microbands emerge earlier (ε_e=0.2) for higher reductions. Slip band refinement saturates at ~50 nm, and finer grains from higher reduction accelerate refinement, causing earlier saturation and microband formation, which shortens stage II and slightly reduces ductility. The conclusions section begins but does not list findings. Key figures: Fig. 9(a,b), Fig. 10, Fig. 11(a-i), Fig. 12; Table 2. Future chunks may elaborate on strain-hardening stages or discuss ductility loss quantitatively.
processing_route: hot rolling at 1150 °C with 45%, 68%, 82% reductions, water quenched
key_variables: yield strength contributions (σ0, σp-κ, σp-(Nb,Mo,W)C, σds, σgb)
microstructure: fully austenitic, equiaxed grains with annealing twin boundaries
planar slip bands and Taylor lattice at low strain
slip band spacing decreases with reduction and strain, saturates at ~50 nm
properties: ductility trends linked to stage II duration
mechanisms: grain boundary strengthening (HAGBs + twin boundaries) predominant
glide plane softening from κ-carbide shearing explains planar glide
dynamic slip band refinement model with back stress on dislocation sources
finer grains accelerate refinement causing earlier microbands and ductility reduction
